title: Food, Cafés & Eating Out Overview
subtitle: Listen to the lesson explained in English

transcript.en: Welcome! 👋 Unit 8 — Food, Cafés and Eating Out.
Four sections this unit: a **café dialogue**, **drinks** vocabulary, **Maltese food**, and **ordering and paying**. Plus a handy grammar point on saying 'with'. Let's get started!
☕ PART 1 — AT THE CAFÉ ☕
Malta runs on coffee. So let's start where everyone starts — the local café. 🇲🇹
The waiter opens with — Good morning! What will you have?
Joseph answers — I'd like a cappuccino, please:
Then the waiter turns to Mary — and you, Mary?
Mary says — for me, a water with lemon, thanks:
Waiter asks — would you like something to eat?
They say yes — two ftiras, please:
Three key ordering phrases from that exchange. Memorise these three and you can order anything:
The phrase for 'I'd like'. The phrase for 'What will you have?' And the word for 'please'. 🙌
🥤 PART 2 — DRINKS 🥤
The drinks menu. Start with the essentials.
Coffee.
Cappuccino — the national drink as far as Maltese café culture is concerned. ☕
Tea.
A cup of coffee.
Coffee with milk.
Water with lemon.
Water.
A bottle of water.
Soft drink.
Orange juice.
Beer.
Wine. 🍷
Twelve drinks. You're ready for any bar or café in Malta. 🎯
🍽️ PART 3 — FOOD 🍽️
Time to eat. Some everyday words and some Maltese classics.
Bread — the foundation of Maltese eating.
The Maltese flatbread — round, crusty, iconic. 🥖
The savoury pastry every visitor discovers in the first hour. 🥐
Ravioli — a Maltese Sunday staple.
Baked pasta pie — thick, rich, completely Maltese.
Rabbit — the national dish. 🐇
Cheeselet — small, fresh, often served with salt and pepper.
Salad.
Soup.
Ice cream. 🍦
You'll see these on menus across Malta. Knowing their names means you can order with confidence — no pointing at the menu required.
🧾 PART 4 — ORDERING AND PAYING 🧾
The essential phrase set for the whole dining experience. 🍴
To order, you have three options. The polite 'I'd like':
The casual 'I'll have':
And 'for me' — when the waiter is going round the table:
To ask the price:
How much is it? To ask for the bill:
The bill, please. And when the food arrives and it's great:
Delicious! 😋
📖 GRAMMAR NOTE — BIL- 📖
One tidy grammar point this unit: how to say 'with'. It uses a little prefix.
Before most letters, that prefix stays as is:
Coffee with milk.
Water with lemon.
But before a 'Xemxin' consonant — like z — it assimilates, just like the article does:
Cappuccino with sugar. The z doubles. Same logic you learned in Unit 1 with the definite article. Pattern recognised! 🧠
✅ DONE!
Café dialogue. Drinks. Maltese food. Ordering and paying. Plus the little word for 'with'. That's Unit 8.
Next time you're at a Maltese snack bar, try ordering in Maltese. You've got the words. 💪
Bye for now! 🇲🇹
